caption:
True…but there’s a plot twist. 

According to Yale School of Medicine, during pregnancy, biological age increased by about 2 years. It makes sense. The sheer metabolic and physiological demands of growing a human naturally accelerates aging at the cellular level.⁠
⁠
But here’s the surprise. At three months postpartum, women’s biological age didn’t just stop increasing, it completely declined. For some, their biological age dropped by as much as 8 years compared to late pregnancy, which can be attributed to: ⁠
⁠
✨ Body Mass Index (BMI): Starting your pregnancy at a higher weight can make it harder for your cells to recover.⁠
⁠
✨ Breastfeeding: Exclusively breastfeeding can biologically fast-forward your recovery.⁠
⁠
The unknown: Lead researcher Kieran O’Donnell noted that they aren’t sure if the body is just returning to its pre-pregnancy baseline or if pregnancy leaves you biologically younger than when you started. ⁠
⁠
Only time will tell. ⁠

caption:
Why I upped my creatine dose to 10 grams per day.

I used to take 5 grams of creatine daily, which provides solid benefits for muscle strength and performance (when combined with resistance training). Recently, I increased my dose to 10 grams, and here’s why I made the switch.

Research suggests that 10 grams of creatine per day may offer additional advantages, particularly for brain health. Studies show that this higher dose increases creatine levels in areas like the grey matter, white matter, and thalamus more effectively than lower doses, such as 4 grams, over an 8-week period. This is likely due to a “spillover effect”, where excess creatine benefits the brain once muscles are fully saturated.

While 5 grams is effective for physical performance, 10 grams seems to provide a broader range of benefits, including potential cognitive support and bone health.

If you’re considering creatine supplementation and want to maximize its effects, a 10-gram daily dose could be worth exploring.

caption:
Most people find that eating close to bed sabotages their sleep quality. Avoiding late-night snacking and having an early dinner appears to resolve this issue.

A common cause of insomnia is an elevated body temperature, and glucose elevations (and a subsequent rise in insulin) increase body temperature through sympathetic nervous system activation.

Melatonin is also involved. And it’s a two-way street. Melatonin levels naturally rise 3 hours before bedtime. High melatonin levels in the evening can inhibit insulin release and its subsequent action. High glucose levels then disrupt the release of melatonin.

A practical, evidence-based approach to optimizing sleep and metabolic function is to finish eating at least 3–4 hours before bed. This early time-restricted feeding aligns with circadian biology, stabilizing blood glucose, improving sleep quality, and enhancing overall metabolic health.

For more insights on optimizing meal timing, sleep quality, and metabolic health, listen to my full conversation with @benbikmanphd.

In light of recent news, it’s worth noting that the link between acetaminophen (e.g., Tylenol) use during pregnancy and childhood behavioral issues is not new. The FDA’s advisory highlights an important association with autism and ADHD that’s been evident in research for years.

A study from 2016 found a 30% increase in the risk of hyperactivity symptoms among children (both genders) exposed to acetaminophen during pregnancy. In boys, exposure was also linked to autism spectrum disorder symptoms in a frequency-dependent manner.

Causation remains unestablished, but it’s exciting that OBGYNs will be getting some guidance on this increased risk to inform women.

It’s also important to remember that autism isn’t from a single cause. There are many factors that may contribute, including:

• Maternal inflammation and infections
• Vitamin D deficiency (affecting serotonin synthesis)
• Low omega-3 status (impacting brain development)
• Metabolic issues (insulin resistance, obesity
• BPA and plastic chemical
• Sperm epigenetic factors

...just to name a few.

In fact, some of my earlier papers reviewed how omega-3 and vitamin D may influence autism risk via serotonin and brain development.

Here are a couple of my earlier papers on how omega-3 and vitamin D may influence autism risk via serotonin and brain development:

caption:
Syrniki reinvented😜
One of my favourite Slavic desserts is Syrniki. It is made with curd cheese. They usually give them a round, slightly flattened shape and fry them in the pan one by one, but I decided to give Syrniki a European touch and baked them in cupcake cases in the oven. Less hassle, more enjoyment😉 

The recipe is very easy. The only thing is the curd cheese itself which you can buy only in Slavic shops in London. Brits don't usually have it.
I bought 2 packs (250gr each) of Polish curd cheese, they call it twarog, which is the same in other Slavic languages. 1 egg, 3-4 tb. spoons of erythritol (I don't use sugar any more to keep my blood sugar levels normal), 3 tb. spoons of oat flour, and a dash of vanilla syrup. Mix it with a blender, and that's it!
Then you just put the mixture into the cupcake cases and bake in the oven(180° for 25 mins). Take them out and let to cool down. Turn them over and serve with any topping. My fav is soured cream. This recipe makes 12 syrniks. Enjoy! 😁😋😊✨️✨️
Переосмысление сырников😜
Один из моих любимых славянских десертов — сырники. Он готовится из творога. Обычно им придают круглую, слегка приплюснутую форму и обжаривают на сковороде по одному, но я решила придать сырникам европейский колорит и запекла их в формочках для кексов в духовке. Меньше хлопот, больше удовольствия😉 

Рецепт очень простой. Единственное, что нужно — это сам творог, который можно купить только в славянских магазинах в Лондоне. У британцев его обычно нет.
Я купила 2 пачки (по 250 г) польского творога, его называют twarog, что то же самое и в других славянских языках. 1 яйцо, 3-4 ст. ложки эритритола (я больше не использую сахар, чтобы поддерживать нормальный уровень сахара в крови), 3 ст. ложки овсяной муки и немного ванильного сиропа. Смешиваю блендером, и все!
 Затем просто положите смесь в формочки для кексов и выпекайте в духовке (180° в течение 25 минут). Достаньте их и дайте остыть. Переверните и подавайте с любой начинкой. Мой любимый вариант — сметана. По этому рецепту получается 12 сырников. Наслаждайтесь! 😁😋😊✨️✨️

GRIP STRENGTH & LONGEVITY: “LIVE HANGS” 

Congratulations Ann Crile Esselstyn, who at *90 years and 231 days* old (title card says 93), did a continual bar grip hang for 2 minutes and 52 seconds. (And set a @guinnessworldrecords WR)

Grip strength and grip endurance (not exactly the same thing) are correlates of longevity. They are great to train for but grip strength alone does not extend life. So what do they reflect?

We often think about the muscle aspect of grip. To me,the really interesting data = the relationship between grip and longevity of *cognitive functioning*.

Why would there be a relationship there?

In neuroscience, we refer to the upper and the lower motor neurons. Upper motor neurons are in the brain and are involved in deliberate action (among other things); lower motor neurons are in the spinal cord and directly connect to the muscles by extending out of the central nervous system into the peripheral nervous system. They directly cause muscles to contract.

We also think about proximal muscles, which are the muscle muscles near the midline and distal muscles like those tips of your calves, forearms, fingers and toes.

Early but compelling evidence suggest that distal strength— the motor neuron control of the parts of your body furthest from your midline, is one of the first things to atrophy as you age. 

You can offset this with strong, gripping, keeping your feet, strong, calves, etc.

So while it’s important to train your core and the muscles of your legs, chest back and arms, don’t forget about feet yes feet! 

And hands and forearms. It’s not just about aesthetics. It’s very likely about maintaining those upper motor brain neurons. 

Once specific sets of neurons in the brain start to die off they often take others with them, the so-called bystander effect.

More on this in a Huberman Lab podcast to come but meanwhile, wherever you’re at in your fitness in longevity training the whole body and keep going! And congrats to Ann. That’s a super impressive timing at any age! 

Please put any questions you have below and thank you for your interest in science!

caption:
STIMULATE MUSCLE GROWTH PROPERLY: 
BACK DAY WITH @thedorianyates (6 time Mr. Olympia) •

Comment BACK & I’ll DM you a link to a video of the full workout @goldsgym.venice 

There are a lot of studies and examples of different ways to train with weights for muscle growth and strength. Which is best? 

Everyone has different opinions on that but what works best for me has been low volume high intensity training. (And when I say “best”, I mean getting the results of directed muscle growth and strength improvements and not having my nervous system feel so fried that I can’t do other things after training… after all, we have lives to live!)

It involves training 3 days per week & each muscle directly 1X per week. Yes only once. You do 1 light set & 1 moderate set just to get the mechanics and blood flow going, then 1 (maybe 2) all out sets to failure *in excellent form* which means controlling the weight all the way up, contracting it hard and lowering it slowly. Depending on the muscle group, you do anywhere from 2 to 4 exercises per muscle group total. 

6 time Mr. Olympia @thedorianyates is my guest on the Huberman Lab podcast out now and we discussed this style of training and the types of training that work best for the everyday person. That means men & women of all ages. He gives a wealth of info.

Dorian was also kind enough to take me through his version of low volume high intensity training. I got to feel what it is to target the back and rear delt muscles properly with true high intensity.

We did:

1) lat pullovers: one light set, one medium set, and then one all outset to failure (w/ a few assisted repetitions).

2) reverse grip cable pull downs (also one set to failure)

3) one arm dumbbell rows (one set to failure for each side)

4) seated wide grip cable rows (one set to failure with a couple of assisted reps)

5) rear delt bent over dumbbell raises (one warm-up and one works set to failure).

Coffee drinkers are biologically younger than those who abstain—and that could help explain why people who drink 2–3 cups per day have up to a 27% lower risk of dying from any cause.

While this doesn’t necessarily mean coffee causes a longer life, there’s compelling biological plausibility behind the link, much of it rooted in epigenetics.

Research shows that coffee influences DNA methylation at 11 specific CpG sites tied to inflammation, metabolism, and age-related diseases. These changes are likely driven by coffee’s rich mix of bioactive compounds, particularly chlorogenic acids, which can act on the cellular machinery that regulates gene expression.

Several studies have also found that moderate coffee intake—about 1 to 3 cups daily—is associated with slower biological aging, reflected in reduced epigenetic and PhenoAge acceleration. On average, each daily cup corresponds to a 0.12-year reduction in epigenetic age.

In other words, regular coffee consumption seems to slow the ticking of our biological clocks, offering one possible explanation for the lower rates of cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and neurodegeneration observed in habitual coffee drinkers.

Lean more about coffee’s effects on aging, optimal brewing methods, and more on our latest episode!

Plastic blenders are a source of microplastics that most people overlook.

Those with plastic jars or pitchers release microplastics, and even nanoplastics, into blended food or drinks due to friction and mechanical abrasion during blending.

A single 30-second blending cycle can release up to 1 billion (yes, with a “b”) micro- and nanoplastic particles.

BPA-free products are safer but can still release particles due to heavy use, heat, or abrasion. My advice is to switch to a completely stainless-steel blender. It’s the only way to avoid contamination.

caption:
Vitamin D supplementation was associated with a 40% lower risk of dementia over a decade.

After five years, 84% of supplement users were dementia-free compared to just 68% of non-users in a study of over 12,000 people. Vitamin D reduced dementia risk by 33% in adults with mild cognitive impairment or APOE e4, a key genetic risk factor for neurodegenerative diseases.

And while vitamin D reduced dementia risk across the board, some groups benefitted more.

Women, adults with normal cognition, APOE e4 non-carriers, and those without depression saw the greatest brain-protective effects from vitamin D supplementation.

Vitamin D isn’t just a vitamin—it’s a steroid hormone regulating over 2,000 genes critical for brain development, neuroplasticity, and inflammation control. Its roles include clearing amyloid beta plaques and tau buildup, boosting neuron survival via neurotrophic factors like BDNF, and enhancing brain connectivity, particularly when paired with exercise.

What can you do to optimize your levels?
• Get sunlight exposure (more details in the video).
• Consider supplementing with vitamin D (2,000 to 4,000 IU/day).
• Aim for 40–80 ng/mL if you’re tracking with a blood test.

To learn more about optimizing vitamin D levels for cognitive health, including underlying brain-protective mechanisms and practical supplementation protocols, see the latest FoundMyFitness video. Links in story.

A new year is not just another day and trip around the sun. A unique feature of the human brain is that we don’t just hold memories of past and perceive things in the present, we can anticipate things far into the future and carry out plans according to how we think we’ll feel in the future. 
-
New Year’s resolutions are good example of us segmenting time and making plans. 
-
I think that’s great. I add one “do more of X” and one “eliminate Y”, each year. (Sometimes several of each, and I write them down, and I check back at the end of the year to see if I held up to it.) 
-
I was struck by a post from @danielpink who’s an expert in the data on behavioral change. He mentioned that one word thematic titles about a new year and what we want from ourselves in that year can have a bigger impact than an unframed list of changes, but it’s good to have a list of specific changes as well. 
-
Apparently a one word thematics can help frame it all and keep you on track. That makes sense; we all get busy… within a few weeks everyone will be talking about Valentine’s Day or whatever and the notion that we’re in a new year seems to vanish. Returning to a one word theme is doable.
-
So… I’m on board this one word thematic idea even if it’s just to remind us of the specific changes to make.

The Sun is rising for me
The Moon is shining for me
The wind is blowing for me
The rain is raining for me
The snow is snowing for me
The sea is breathing for me
The sand is getting warm for me
The flowers are blooming for me

The music is created for me to dance to it

To kiss your child, to hug your lover, to be held by your parent, to laugh with your best friend- all of that is so tempting you can't resist it. Feel free to borrow that note and keep it as a reminder. 
Don't waist your time, enjoy all of that while you can, even though there's underlying bitterness of a loss present in every second of your life here.

caption:
Nicotine can enhance cognition, but it comes at a cost to cardiovascular health.

A single 2–4 mg nicotine pouch can raise blood pressure by ~5 mmHg and increase resting heart rate by ~5–10 beats per minute. Higher doses lead to more pronounced elevations.

More concerning is nicotine’s effect on arterial stiffness, which is a marker strongly associated with long-term cardiovascular disease risk.

These effects are driven largely by nicotine’s activation of the sympathetic nervous system, which promotes vasoconstriction and impairs endothelial function.

While the cardiovascular effects are often short-lived, repeated exposure can make them persistent in habitual users.

I talked about nicotine as well as supplements for cancer, inflammation, and general health and well-being with @missmayim on her podcast earlier this year.

caption:
This past week I stepped away from my normal routine and spent a couple days hiking through the Grand Canyon and the red rock desert of Sedona with my family. No notifications. No news cycle. Just thousands of feet of elevation change, warm sun, cold mornings, tired legs, and a very visceral reminder of how precious it is to have a body and a family healthy enough to do this together. On a day that is about gratitude, I am thankful for that.

Long nature hikes have many health benefits. Mechanical loading on muscles and bones, mitochondrial biogenesis driven by energetic demands, intense natural light exposure resetting circadian rhythms, and visually immersive natural environments quieting the brain’s rumination-prone default-mode network.

Yet, what I value most deeply is the shared effort and hardship with my family, especially teaching my son that strength emerges directly from struggle. There were parts of our hikes that were more challenging and hard. True adaptation requires stress. Stress in the muscles, mitochondria, even the mind itself, all grow stronger in response to challenge. Without hardship, there can be no meaningful growth. This was the most valuable lesson that I continue to instill in my son.

Today I’m especially thankful for my health, for the health of my family, and for the ability to share these kinds of experiences with them. Wishing you and your loved ones a healthy, peaceful, and restorative Thanksgiving.

caption:
Creatine supplementation improved cognitive function by nearly 5% in Alzheimer’s patients over just 8 weeks.

In the first-ever trial of its kind, Alzheimer’s patients supplementing with high-dose creatine (20 grams per day) saw an 11% increase in total brain creatine levels. 

Their cognitive scores improved by 4.4%, driven by substantial gains in working memory, fluid cognition, inhibitory control and attention, and oral reading recognition. And while creatine boosted cognition broadly, the greatest improvements correlated with higher increases in brain creatine stores.

Creatine’s cognitive benefits likely stem from its critical role in brain energy metabolism, addressing core bioenergetic deficits linked to Alzheimer’s like dysfunctional mitochondria, impaired glucose metabolism, reduced ATP, and disrupted brain creatine systems (hallmarks of disease that creatine supplementation appears to counteract).

These promising results position creatine as an inexpensive and safe therapeutic option, potentially slowing Alzheimer’s disease progression when combined with broader lifestyle and neuroprotective strategies.

caption:
Beware of most creatine gummies. Recent third-party testing found that 2/3 of creatine gummy brands contained virtually no creatine, despite label claims.

Several brands advertised 5 g of creatine per serving, but testing found most delivered <0.5% of the stated amount. Only two brands actually matched (or exceeded) what they claimed.

Gummies are often a bad delivery system in general, not just for creatine. Manufacturing heat and processing can degrade sensitive compounds and reduce biological activity. You’re also paying for sugar, fillers, and convenience, not potency.

Plus, if you’re aiming for a meaningful daily dose (e.g., 10–20 g/day), that’s a lot of gummies.

Creatine monohydrate powder is still the gold standard for those who want to supplement.

THE ONE HEALTH PROTOCOL THAT MAKES ALL THE REST FAR EASIER: SPIKE YOUR CORTISOL IN THE 1ST HOUR OF YOUR DAY •
-
Guess what, whether you are male or female, young or old you want your morning cortisol high (yes high) and your nighttime cortisol low. That is what’s going to establish healthy metabolism, focus, excellent sleep, best stress control, even heightened ability to learn and longevity. 
-
Deliberately spiking your morning cortisol the way I describe in this video will help you have low cortisol in the final hours before sleep, and will vastly improve your sleep. And as we all know, high-quality sleep is the foundation of mental health, physical health, and performance. 
-
Cortisol has gotten a bad rap.  It’s healthy function is to wake you up each morning. It’s literally called. The cortisol awakening response. And if you don’t spike your cortisol in the first hours of your day, you make yourself vulnerable to cortisol increases later in the day, more stress and hormone dysregulation as well as sleep disturbances.
-
Also: if you are a regular drinker of caffeine, know that it does not increase cortisol. 
-
Also this: deliberate cold exposure does not increase cortisol! The best studies show the exact opposite! 
-
In this video, I explain simple zero cost things you can do to get your cortisol rhythm correct. Viewing bright light ideally from sunlight in the first hour of your day is going to be the most potent tool for that, but after an hour post waking that window closes at least until the next morning. 10K Lux artificial light is helpful if you can’t access sunlight.
-

WHAT HAPPENS IF YOU DO NOT FOLLOW YOUR DREAMS •
-
My guest on the Huberman lab podcast out now is Steven Pressfield, author of numerous best selling books and screenplays. We discussed how to overcome resistance and procrastination using practical tools, not mantras or inspirational language.
-
Steven wrote his first book at 52 (!) and at 82 is still incredibly mentally and physically vigorous (!) He resistance trains 5 days a week at 4:45 AM, and approaches his writing with the same rigor. 2 hours a day every day. No exceptions.
-
Here we are discussing what happens if you do not pursue your dreams. And while that might seem like a luxury, there are numerous accounts of people finding an hour or two per day to write or draw or do things to build out their creative dreams.
-
His point is not meant to scare people into doing what they love, but rather to consider seriously what they most want and to make the pockets of time to pursue that. 
-
His point that the energy we are endowed with to make our own unique contribution to the world is a sort of divine energy is one I subscribe to… and I agree that if we don’t channel it correctly, it can end up being destructive.
-
The episode is filled with practical tools for very busy people that work.
-
The episode can be found on all platforms: YouTube, Apple Podcasts, Spotify, etc. and is linked out to all these at Hubermanlab.com

caption:
Rates of colorectal cancer incidence have steadily risen in the last few decades, and I think that ultra-processed foods, particularly processed meats, are a major culprit. 

Along with cancer rates, processed meat intake has also increased. Processed meat accounts for approximately 25% of all meat consumed in the U.S. 

In 2015, the International Agency for Research on Cancer classified processed meat (bacon, sausages, hot dogs, deli meat) as a Group 1 carcinogen, meaning there’s strong evidence it raises cancer risk. Each 50-gram daily serving—about two slices of deli ham—raises colorectal cancer risk by 18%.

Processed meats are specifically those preserved by curing, salting, smoking, or fermenting, producing carcinogens like N-nitroso compounds and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. Simple mechanical processing (such as grass-fed beef burgers) doesn’t carry the same risk, though high-heat cooking still generates carcinogenic compounds.

Beyond processed meats, diets high in ultra-processed foods as a whole, including packaged breads, sugary drinks, and ready-to-eat meals, carry an 11% higher colorectal cancer hazard overall, and a 29% higher risk in men, likely driven by microbiome-disrupting emulsifiers, added sugars, and chronic low-grade inflammation.

To practically lower colorectal cancer risk, limit ultra-processed foods and prioritize nutrient-dense whole foods.

caption:
Receipts are lined with BPA. One of the simplest ways to lower your exposure to this endocrine-disrupting chemical is to avoid handling them.

It might feel trivial, but repeated exposure adds up. Cashiers and others who handle receipts daily have much higher BPA levels in their urine—evidence that BPA is absorbed through the skin while working.

Crucially, nitrile gloves (not latex) block BPA absorption. If you’re handling receipts regularly, they’re a must. Hand creams and sanitizers can increase BPA absorption up to 100-fold.

The practical takeaway here is apparent: If it’s not your job, skip the paper receipt and choose electronic instead. Small, consistent habits like this reduce your total chemical burden more than you might think.

OK, we have to be clear that blue light does not necessarily need to appear blue. I know that’s confusing but what we’re talking about is short wavelength light: and people use the term blue light as a proxy for that. 
-
Incandescent bulbs tend to be full spectrum meaning they emit short, medium, and long wavelengths. That is good because the long wavelengths improve your mitochondria and offset what appears to be the damage of short wavelengths. 
-
Sunlight, of course, is full spectrum. So getting sunlight in your eyes and on your skin each day can help offset some of the mitochondrial damage being done by LED bulbs. 

It’s also good for you in a number of other ways, including hormone health, energy, skin health, eyesight and much more. Obviously you don’t want a sunburn so don’t over expose yourself to UV. The best way to avoid doing that is to just check the UV index in your area.
-
Red light and infrared light devices can be very helpful, but are not necessary. If you can use incandescent bulbs at home do it but as a general rule: try and make your days as bright with sunlight as you can without getting a sunburn get outside in the am if possible and then work near a window if you can.

Supplementing with protein during strength training can increase gains in muscle size by approximately 27%, even when baseline protein intake is already considered adequate.

The current RDA for protein, 0.8 grams per kilogram of body weight per day, is meant to prevent deficiency, not to optimize muscle growth or performance. Increasing intake to 1.5x the RDA leads to greater improvements in both muscle size and strength when combined with resistance exercise.

Doubling the RDA is even better. Meta-analyses suggest that around 1.6 g/kg/day of protein is sufficient to maximize gains in muscle strength and fat-free mass, with potential (though smaller) benefits observed at higher intakes up to 2.2 g/kg/day for those aiming to fully optimize muscle growth and recovery (like athletes).

This highlights how conservative the RDA truly is and why higher protein intakes are important, particularly for individuals engaging in regular strength training or other forms of physical activity.

Having your last meal at least 3 hours before bed improves blood pressure, heart health, and even blood glucose regulation.

That was without changing what or how much they ate!

What you eat matters. But so does when.

Water from glass bottles might contain up to 3x more microplastics than plastic bottles.

A recent study tested common beverages in France and consistently found glass bottles had the highest microplastic contamination, significantly exceeding plastic bottles and cans.

Beer topped the list, with small glass bottles averaging 134 microplastics per liter (MPs/L). Lemonades (112 MPs/L), colas (103 MPs/L), and cold teas (86 MPs/L) followed (all in glass), with far fewer MPs (1.5–2.4 MPs/L) in plastic bottles or cans.

Even plain bottled water wasn’t exempt. Glass bottles averaged 4.5 MPs/L. That’s 181% higher than plastic bottles, which averaged 1.6 MPs/L.

Most surprising of all? The contamination wasn’t from the glass itself. Rather, it came from the bottle caps. Researchers discovered that flakes of polyester-based paint on metal caps shed into the beverages. Uncleaned caps drove contamination levels as high as 287 MPs/L, while cleaning caps greatly reduced microplastic contamination by up to 70%.

It really seems that, as a consumer, there is no easy way out. So what can you do?

Filtered water from a reusable, BPA-free bottle remains your best choice to limit exposure. And recognize potential trade-offs. While glass may contain more microplastic particles, plastic bottles (especially when exposed to heat) generally test higher for PFAS and endocrine-disrupting chemicals like BPA and BPS. 

_ 

From my recent appearance on The Diary of a CEO (@thediaryofaceopodcast)

caption:
Dad’s workouts may shape his child’s endurance capacity and metabolic health.

Not by passing on mitochondria—which come from mom—but by sending tiny sperm microRNA messages that tune early embryonic gene regulation.

In a new study (mice), exercising fathers produced offspring with a higher VO2 max, greater endurance, a leaner body composition, and more mitochondria. They also had superior glucose control and insulin sensitivity when challenged with a high-fat diet.

Effects tracked to sperm small RNAs that suppressed a “molecular brake” on PGC-1α, the classic mitochondrial biogenesis switch.

This reframes preconception health as a two-parent story: mom supplies the mitochondria, but dad’s training status can still program how those mitochondria are used.

Study referenced - DOI: 10.1016/j.cmet.2025.09.003

caption:
Daily supplementation with 2,000 IU of vitamin D  slows the rate of telomere shortening, preserving telomere length equivalent to delaying ~3 years of biological aging over four years.

Telomeres are protective DNA sequences that cap the ends of chromosomes and are critical for preventing genomic instability. Their shortening is closely linked to aging and an increased susceptibility to chronic diseases. 

Mechanistically, vitamin D may exert these anti-aging effects by increasing telomerase activity, reducing DNA damage, and modulating inflammatory pathways via vitamin D receptor–dependent mechanisms. 

The VITAL Telomere sub-study was a rigorous, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial that assessed telomere length in leukocytes from 1,054 adults aged 50 years and older over a 4-year period.

Maintaining serum vitamin D levels of 40–60 ng/mL through daily supplementation is a reliable, evidence-based strategy to slow telomere shortening and potentially delay biological aging.

Study PMID: 40409468

caption:
WANT TO LEARN FASTER? UNDERSTANDING HOW NEUROPLASTICITY WORKS IS THE KEY & IT INVOLVES 4 (actually 5) CRITICAL STEPS •
-
ALL learning involves neuroplasticity, and while a lot has been discussed about this topic, most of what you see misses the mark in one or several ways.
-
To learn, 1) you need to be alert, and 2) you need to be focused. But those are just prerequisites. They are necessary, but not sufficient for learning. The critical step that triggers the actual rewiring of brain circuits that causes “learning” is… 3) ERRORS.
-
Errors and often the emotional frustration that accompanies them is what signals to your nervous system that something needs to change. It’s critical that you internalize that fact! It makes logical sense if you think about it. Why would your nervous system change if it could perform everything that you’re trying to do?
-
4) Actual circuit remodeling underlying learning occurs during sleep, in particular during rapid eye movement sleep (REM).
-
And then there’s the additional critical feature that most people overlook and that’s 5) mental reflection on the learning process and in particular self testing for what you did correctly and what you did incorrectly.
-
In fact, the data on how best to study and learn all point to self testing as an incredibly valuable, but under used tool.
-
In this post, I discussed the practical steps to trigger and enhance learning. I also discussed how to accelerate learning and how to make it more durable over time. It’s 4 key steps. (And one additional one to boost learning).
-
Perhaps test yourself and see if you can remember what those 4-5 steps are… if you can’t don’t worry, go back and read it again. Yes this is an example of what I’m trying to explain. But once it’s locked in, you’ll never forget it.

caption:
Living near a golf course may substantially elevate your risk of Parkinson’s disease, according to new research linking geographic proximity, pesticide exposure, and drinking-water contamination.

Living within 1 mile of a golf course increased Parkinson’s risk by 126% compared to living over 6 miles away, and the greatest risk (a 198% increase) was observed for those living 1–2 miles from a golf course.

People relying on groundwater near golf courses had nearly double the risk of Parkinson’s compared to areas without golf courses. Putting aside proximity to golf courses, an 82–90% risk arises in areas with vulnerable groundwater (easier pesticide contamination).

This elevated risk likely arises from pesticides commonly applied on golf courses, notably paraquat and rotenone, which impair mitochondria and induce oxidative stress, selectively damaging dopamine-producing neurons—a hallmark of Parkinson’s pathology.

While the study’s observational nature prevents definitive conclusions about causation, the findings highlight environmental pesticide exposure as a significant and actionable modifiable risk factor for Parkinson’s disease.

A practical take-home measure for those living near a golf course is to use a water filter, such as a reverse osmosis water filter.

I covered this study in my weekly newsletter. Make sure to subscribe if you’re interested in learning about topics like this.

caption:
Micro-dosing GLP-1 receptor agonists (drugs like semaglutide/Ozempic) could offer meaningful benefits while minimizing harmful side effects.

There’s no doubt these drugs are incredibly effective for weight loss. But the high doses currently being used might be too much of a good thing.

For example, high-dose GLP-1 receptor agonist use has been linked with a near doubling in the risk of suicidal behavior and a tripling in the risk of depression, as well as a two-fold increase in the risk of age-related macular degeneration.

However, these drugs may still have utility when used strategically. According to Dr. Ben Bikman (@benbikmanphd), micro-dosing GLP-1 receptor agonists intermittently may be a beneficial strategy to manage hunger, reset eating behaviors, and encourage healthier dietary patterns. Specifically, Bikman proposes that using low doses for about 90 days could fundamentally shift one’s relationship with food.

There is even some speculation that these medications may have pro-longevity benefits, independent of their effects on weight loss.

To learn more, check out the new FMF episode with Dr. Ben Bikman.

caption:
Recent research is showing that some of these weight loss drugs GLP-1 receptor agonists, which include Ozempic and things like Wegovy may decrease cancer mortality. The people who are most obese benefit the most.

The effects were big.

Colon cancer patients taking GLP-1 drugs (Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro) were less than half as likely to die within 5 years.

This new study suggests GLP-1 drugs might specifically target obesity-driven inflammatory and metabolic pathways known to accelerate colon cancer progression.

There are several mechanisms by which GLP-1 agonists may suppress tumor growth, including weight loss, improving glucose regulation and insulin sensitivity, and lowering inflammation.

There is also some interesting in-vitro cell culture data indicating that these drugs may directly slow cancer cell proliferation, induce apoptosis, and remodel the tumor microenvironment itself.

So what’s the take-away? Should everyone be on these drugs? Absolutely not. Weight loss is critical. Ideally, diet and lifestyle changes would drive it, but clearly, we’re losing that fight in the US, where obesity contributes to at least 13 types of cancer and many other diseases.

In this study, the greatest benefit from GLP-1 drugs was seen in people with obesity, exactly those who struggle most to lose weight through lifestyle alone.

I think the GLP-1s represent a potential tool, particularly when traditional methods have repeatedly failed some people.

Study title:
The Influence of GLP-1 Receptor Agonists on Five-Year Mortality in Colon Cancer Patients

caption:
The top 10 strategies to reduce cancer risk:

1. Avoid tobacco - Smoking causes 80-90% of lung cancer deaths; quitting reduces excess risk by 90% if done early and by 50% within 10-15 years even if done later in life.

2. Maintain a healthy body weight - Obesity significantly elevates risk for 13 cancers, including breast, colon, and endometrial cancers; modest weight loss substantially reduces this risk.

3. Engage in regular physical activity - Aim for ≥150 minutes/week vigorous or ≥300 minutes/week moderate-intensity exercise. Exercise reduces inflammation and insulin resistance, critical drivers of cancer growth.

4. Diet

Fiber - Diets high in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and legumes—and low in processed meats—reduce colorectal cancer risk by up to 60%.

Limit intake of processed meat and ultra-processed food - Regular intake of processed meats containing nitrates and preservatives is strongly linked to colorectal cancer. 

5. Reduce alcohol intake - Even moderate daily drinking increases breast cancer risk (~10% per drink/day) while heavy drinking elevates oral and throat cancer risk five-fold.

6. Protect against excessive UV radiation - Regular sunscreen use (SPF ≥30) reduces melanoma incidence by ~50% over 10 years.

7. Mitigate harmful environmental exposures - Air pollution, secondhand smoke, and radon significantly increase lung cancer risk.

8. Manage stress - Chronic stress elevates cortisol and inflammation, impairing immune surveillance. Robust observational data link prolonged stress to increased cancer incidence.

9. Optimize sleep and circadian rhythms - Poor sleep and disrupted circadian rhythms (e.g., shift work) are classified by WHO as probable carcinogens.

10. Supplement considerations

Vitamin D - Clinical data associate vitamin D supplementation with reduced cancer mortality, especially among those genetically predisposed to low levels.

Magnesium - Deficiency is common (~50% US adults). Supplementation helps maintain immune and metabolic health and help with DNA repair (magnesium is a cofactor for DNA repair enzymes), indirectly and directly supporting cancer prevention.

caption:
Using the sauna after aerobic exercise improves VO₂ max more than training alone.

People who performed 30 minutes of cycling and then sat in a sauna for ~15 minutes afterward saw greater gains in their VO₂ max after 8 weeks of training compared to those who did the workout without sauna.

There’s also emerging evidence for strength training, with studies showing greater increases in markers of anabolic signaling associated with muscle growth with post-exercise heat exposure.

The sauna has several health benefits. But it’s also a powerful tool to amplify the body’s adaptations to both endurance and strength training.

caption:
When it comes to breast cancer, the evidence is clear: Zero alcohol is safest.

Alcohol is a known cause of female breast cancer. Just one drink a day raises a woman’s lifetime risk by about 4%, and the risk climbs with each additional drink. 

There’s no safe threshold. It’s a dose-response relationship - the more you drink, the higher the risk.

Given that 1 in 8 women will be diagnosed with breast cancer in their lifetime, even a small increase in risk is meaningful. And for women with genetic susceptibility or health factors like obesity, alcohol’s impact can be even greater.

Knowing this empowers you to make informed choices about your health—choices grounded in the science instead of wishful thinking or speculation about a “safe” amount.
